articles.headline: Under-Construction Delhi-Mumbai Expressway Tunnel Collapses in Kota, Raising Infrastructure Safety Concerns
articles.explainer:
• At least one person was killed and three others injured in the collapse of an under-construction tunnel section of the Delhi–Mumbai Expressway in Kota, Rajasthan.
• The incident highlights critical issues regarding safety protocols, quality control, and supervision in large-scale infrastructure projects across the country.
• Authorities have initiated an inquiry to ascertain the cause, including potential lapses in design, construction practices, or material quality.
• The Delhi-Mumbai Expressway is a flagship project aimed at reducing travel time and boosting economic activity across several states.
• This incident brings to the forefront the need for stringent regulatory oversight and comprehensive risk assessment in major public works.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Disaster and disaster management

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention
prelimsPoints: Delhi-Mumbai Expressway: Key features, states covered (Rajasthan, MP, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Haryana).
NHAI: Mandate and role in highway development.
Disaster Management Act, 2005: Provisions for accident response.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in ensuring safety standards in large-scale infrastructure projects in India, using the Kota incident as a case study.
Analyze the role of regulatory bodies and project contractors in maintaining quality and safety during construction.
Suggest measures to improve disaster preparedness and response mechanisms for construction site accidents.
articles.imageDescription: Collapsed section of an under-construction tunnel of the Delhi-Mumbai Expressway.
articles.trivia: The Delhi-Mumbai Expressway, once completed, will be India's longest greenfield expressway, reducing travel time between Delhi and Mumbai from 24 hours to 12 hours.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Reinforces Right to Privacy in Digital Age, Issues Guidelines on State Surveillance
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, reiterated the fundamental right to privacy, extending its scope to cover digital data and online activities.
• The judgment emphasized that any state-sanctioned surveillance or data collection must meet strict tests of legality, necessity, and proportionality.
• While acknowledging national security concerns, the court stressed the need for robust oversight mechanisms to prevent arbitrary infringement of privacy.
• The ruling comes amidst growing debates over data protection laws and the balance between individual liberties and state powers in the digital era.
• It calls for a re-evaluation of existing legal frameworks governing surveillance and data handling by government agencies.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functioning of the Judiciary
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: K.S. Puttaswamy judgment (2017) and its significance.
Article 21 of the Indian Constitution (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
Key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on the balance between national security and individual privacy in India's digital landscape.
Discuss the challenges in implementing effective data protection and surveillance oversight mechanisms in a technologically evolving society.
Analyze how this judgment could influence future government policies on digital governance and data regulation.
articles.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with a digital overlay representing data privacy.
articles.trivia: The 'Right to be Forgotten' is an aspect of the right to privacy, allowing individuals to request the removal of personal information from the internet under certain conditions.

articles.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Surpasses Projections, Signals Strong Economic Recovery
articles.explainer:
• India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) registered a robust growth rate of 7.8% in the second quarter (July-September) of FY 2024-25, exceeding market expectations.
• The growth was primarily driven by strong performance in the manufacturing, construction, and services sectors, indicating broad-based recovery.
• Private consumption and government expenditure also contributed significantly to the economic expansion.
• This positive trend strengthens India's position as one of the fastest-growing major economies globally, despite prevailing global headwinds.
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and various international agencies are likely to revise their full-year growth forecasts upwards.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
articles.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation
prelimsPoints: Definition and components of GDP and GVA.
Role of CSO (now NSO) in GDP calculation.
Current trends in key economic indicators (inflation, industrial production).
Impact of global factors on India's economy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's strong Q2 GDP growth and discuss its sustainability in the current global economic scenario.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India to maintain its growth momentum and achieve its long-term economic objectives.
Discuss the implications of this growth on employment generation, poverty reduction, and investment climate in India.
articles.imageDescription: Financial graph showing an upward trend, representing India's GDP growth.
articles.trivia: India's economy is projected to become the third-largest in the world by 2027, surpassing Germany and Japan.

articles.headline: India and France Bolster Strategic Partnership with Focus on Defence, Space, and Clean Energy
articles.explainer:
• Prime Minister of India concluded a successful state visit to France, resulting in several key agreements to deepen the bilateral strategic partnership.
• Major outcomes include enhanced cooperation in defence manufacturing and technology transfer, particularly in naval and aerospace sectors.
• Both nations committed to expanding collaboration in space exploration, including joint missions and satellite technology development.
• Agreements were also signed in the areas of clean energy transition, artificial intelligence, and cybersecurity.
• The visit underscored the shared vision of a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, with emphasis on multilateralism and rules-based international order.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Major defence deals between India and France (e.g., Rafale, Scorpene submarines).
India's key strategic partners in Europe.
Significance of Indo-Pacific concept for India and France.
International Solar Alliance (ISA) and India-France collaboration.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the significance of the India-France strategic partnership in enhancing India's defence capabilities and promoting its foreign policy objectives.
Discuss how India's engagement with France contributes to regional stability in the Indo-Pacific and addresses global challenges like climate change.
Analyze the potential for future cooperation between India and France in emerging technologies and multilateral forums.
articles.imageDescription: Prime Minister of India and President of France shaking hands with flags in the background.
articles.trivia: France was the first country to sign a strategic partnership with India in 1998.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Critical Test for Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle Technology
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful completion of a crucial experimental test flight for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) program.
• The test involved a scaled-down prototype demonstrating autonomous landing capabilities under simulated operational conditions.
• This achievement marks a significant step towards developing India's own two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) reusable launch system, aiming to drastically reduce space mission costs.
• RLV technology is considered vital for future space exploration, satellite deployment, and potential space tourism.
• ISRO aims to achieve full operational capability for its RLV by the end of the decade, enhancing India's competitive edge in the global space market.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Difference between expendable and reusable launch vehicles.
Key ISRO missions (Gaganyaan, Chandrayaan, Mangalyaan).
Role of IN-SPACe and NSIL in India's space sector.
Benefits of reusable launch technology (cost reduction, environmental impact).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of developing Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology for India's space program and its implications for global space competitiveness.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with indigenous development of advanced space technologies like RLV.
How can India leverage its advancements in space technology to foster economic growth and contribute to sustainable development goals?
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle prototype landing on a runway.
articles.trivia: The first experimental flight of ISRO's RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) was conducted in 2016, successfully demonstrating the hypersonic flight regime.

articles.headline: Government Launches National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture to Boost Farmers' Adaptability
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government officially launched the 'National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture' (NMCRA) aimed at enhancing the adaptability of Indian agriculture to climate change.
• The mission focuses on developing and promoting climate-smart agricultural practices, including drought-resistant crop varieties, efficient water management, and sustainable land use.
• It will integrate traditional knowledge with modern scientific approaches to build resilience across various agro-climatic zones.
• NMCRA also includes components for capacity building among farmers, providing access to climate information services, and promoting agroforestry.
• The initiative is crucial for ensuring food security and farmers' livelihoods in the face of increasing climate variability and extreme weather events.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
keyTerms: National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)
prelimsPoints: Eight Missions under NAPCC (National Action Plan on Climate Change).
Key government initiatives for agricultural development (e.g., PM-KISAN, PM Fasal Bima Yojana).
Concepts of 'climate-smart village' and 'precision agriculture'.
Different types of irrigation systems in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture in addressing the challenges posed by climate change to India's food security and agricultural sector.
Analyze the potential impact of climate-smart agricultural practices on farmers' income and environmental sustainability.
Suggest policy measures and technological interventions required to ensure widespread adoption and success of climate-resilient agriculture in India.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with modern agricultural technology and diverse crops.
articles.trivia: India is highly vulnerable to climate change, with agriculture being one of the most susceptible sectors, impacting over 58% of the rural population.

articles.headline: Indian Army Inducts Advanced Indigenous Artillery Systems, Boosting 'Make in India' in Defence
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Army formally inducted a new batch of indigenously developed advanced artillery gun systems, marking a significant milestone for 'Make in India' in defence.
• These new systems, designed and manufactured by Indian firms, feature enhanced range, accuracy, and mobility, crucial for modern warfare scenarios.
• The induction is part of the ongoing modernization drive of the Indian armed forces to reduce reliance on foreign imports and achieve self-reliance in defence production.
• This move is expected to boost the domestic defence industrial base, create jobs, and foster technological advancements within the country.
• The government reiterated its commitment to indigenous defence procurement and exports, positioning India as a global defence manufacturing hub.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Key indigenous defence platforms (e.g., LCA Tejas, Arjun Tank, Akash missile).
Defence corridors in India.
Recent policy changes to boost domestic defence manufacturing.
Role of DRDO in defence R&D.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of indigenous defence production for India's national security and strategic autonomy.
Discuss the challenges faced by India in achieving complete self-reliance in defence manufacturing and suggest measures to overcome them.
Evaluate the impact of 'Make in India' initiatives on the modernization of the Indian armed forces and the growth of the domestic defence industry.
articles.imageDescription: New indigenous artillery guns being showcased by the Indian Army.
articles.trivia: India is one of the largest importers of defence equipment globally, but the 'Make in India' initiative aims to reverse this trend.

articles.headline: Union Cabinet Approves 'PM-POSHAN+' Scheme for Enhanced Nutritional Support to School Children
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet gave its approval for the 'PM-POSHAN+' scheme, an upgraded version of the existing Mid-Day Meal program, focusing on holistic nutritional support for school children.
• The scheme aims to extend its coverage to pre-primary children and incorporate fortified food items to address micronutrient deficiencies.
• It emphasizes nutritional gardens in schools, cooking competitions, and community participation to promote healthy eating habits.
• PM-POSHAN+ also integrates health check-ups and nutritional counseling, linking directly with broader public health initiatives.
• This enhanced scheme is critical in combating malnutrition and improving educational outcomes, aligning with India's Sustainable Development Goals.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Poverty and developmental issues.
prelimsPoints: Objectives and features of the original Mid-Day Meal Scheme (now PM-POSHAN).
Key indicators of child malnutrition in India (stunting, wasting, underweight).
Government initiatives for women and child development.
SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) and SDG 4 (Quality Education).
mainsPoints: Evaluate the effectiveness of the Mid-Day Meal program in addressing child malnutrition and promoting school enrollment in India. How does PM-POSHAN+ aim to improve upon it?
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in combating malnutrition among school children and the role of inter-sectoral convergence in achieving better outcomes.
Suggest innovative approaches for strengthening community participation and ensuring sustainable nutritional support programs in schools.
articles.imageDescription: School children enjoying a nutritious meal in a classroom setting.
articles.trivia: The Mid-Day Meal Scheme was renamed as PM POSHAN (Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Nirman) Scheme in 2021.

articles.headline: Government's PSU Disinvestment Drive Gains Momentum, New Assets Listed for Strategic Sale
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government announced accelerated progress in its Public Sector Undertaking (PSU) disinvestment program, listing several new state-owned assets for strategic sale.
• The move is aimed at unlocking value from government holdings, generating revenue for public welfare schemes, and improving the efficiency and competitiveness of PSUs.
• Key sectors identified for disinvestment include manufacturing, financial services, and logistics, with a focus on non-strategic enterprises.
• The government reiterated its commitment to minimal presence in strategic sectors while privatizing non-strategic ones, as part of its new Public Sector Enterprise Policy.
• Challenges remain in market valuation, investor interest, and employee concerns, requiring careful policy execution and stakeholder management.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
articles.source: Department of Investment and Public Asset Management (DIPAM)
prelimsPoints: Definition of disinvestment and privatization.
Objectives of government's disinvestment policy.
Role of DIPAM (Department of Investment and Public Asset Management).
Concept of strategic vs. non-strategic sectors.
mainsPoints: Critically examine the rationale behind the government's accelerated PSU disinvestment drive and its potential impact on the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges and criticisms associated with privatization and strategic sale of public sector enterprises in India.
Suggest measures to ensure transparency, maximize value realization, and address employee concerns during the disinvestment process.
articles.imageDescription: Indian currency notes stacked on a table, symbolizing revenue generation from disinvestment.
articles.trivia: The first major disinvestment in India took place in 1991-92, marking a shift in economic policy.

articles.headline: India Reiterates Call for Equitable Climate Finance and CBDR at COP29
articles.explainer:
• At the ongoing COP29 climate summit, India strongly advocated for equitable climate finance and upheld the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC).
• India called upon developed nations to fulfill their financial commitments, emphasizing the need for predictable and adequate funding for climate action in developing countries.
• The Indian delegation highlighted its ambitious national targets for renewable energy and emissions reduction, despite being a developing economy.
• Discussions centered on mechanisms for technology transfer, capacity building, and adaptation finance for vulnerable nations.
• India stressed that climate justice is paramount and that the burden of climate action should not disproportionately fall on developing nations.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Location and key outcomes of COP29 (hypothetical, as COP29 is Nov 11-22, 2024).
Principle of CBDR-RC: its origin and significance.
India's NDC targets under the Paris Agreement.
Difference between mitigation and adaptation in climate action.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's stance on climate finance and the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) at international climate forums.
Analyze the challenges in achieving global consensus on climate finance and technology transfer, particularly between developed and developing nations.
Evaluate India's efforts and commitments towards climate action, balancing its development imperatives with environmental sustainability.
articles.imageDescription: Delegates at a COP conference, with a globe overlay representing climate change.
articles.trivia: The Paris Agreement (2015) was the first universal, legally binding global climate change agreement, replacing the Kyoto Protocol.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Comprehensive Reforms in Anti-Defection Law to Ensure Political Stability
articles.explainer:
• A high-level Parliamentary Standing Committee submitted its report recommending significant amendments to the Anti-Defection Law (Tenth Schedule of the Constitution).
• Key recommendations include clarifying the definition of 'voluntary giving up membership' and 'defiance of party whip' to reduce ambiguity.
• The panel suggested a time-bound decision-making process for the Speaker/Chairman on defection petitions, possibly involving the Election Commission or Supreme Court.
• The reforms aim to curb frequent party switching and ensure greater political stability, while also protecting the democratic rights of elected representatives.
• The report sparked renewed debate on the balance between party discipline and legislative freedom, emphasizing the need for robust institutional checks.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: Provisions of the Tenth Schedule of the Indian Constitution.
52nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1985).
Role of the Speaker/Chairman in anti-defection cases.
Exceptions to the anti-defection law (merger).
Kihoto Hollohan vs. Zachillhu case (1992).
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the effectiveness of the Anti-Defection Law in promoting political stability and ensuring party discipline in India. What are its major shortcomings?
Discuss the recommendations made by the Parliamentary Panel for reforming the Anti-Defection Law. How might these changes impact the functioning of Indian democracy?
Evaluate the role of the Speaker/Chairman in deciding defection cases and suggest measures to ensure impartiality and timely disposal of such petitions.
articles.imageDescription: Indian Parliament building with a gavel, representing legislative reforms.
articles.trivia: The Anti-Defection Law was enacted to prevent political defections that often led to instability in governments, especially at the state level.

articles.headline: Western Dedicated Freight Corridor Nearing Completion, Set to Transform Logistics Efficiency
articles.explainer:
• The Western Dedicated Freight Corridor (WDFC), a crucial infrastructure project, is reportedly nearing its full operational completion, with final stretches expected to be commissioned soon.
• WDFC aims to provide a dedicated, high-speed freight rail network connecting the industrial hubs of northern India to the western ports (Jawaharlal Nehru Port, Vadodara, etc.).
• This corridor is expected to significantly reduce transit times, lower logistics costs, and decongest existing passenger rail lines.
• It will boost industrial growth, enhance export competitiveness, and facilitate faster movement of goods, contributing to India's economic development.
• The project is a key component of India's broader National Rail Plan, focused on upgrading and expanding the country's railway infrastructure.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
articles.source: Dedicated Freight Corridor Corporation of India Limited (DFCCIL)
prelimsPoints: Difference between WDFC and EDFC (routes, states covered, cargo types).
Objectives of the Dedicated Freight Corridor project.
Major ports on India's western coast.
Significance of improving logistics efficiency for economic growth.
mainsPoints: Assess the transformative potential of the Western Dedicated Freight Corridor (WDFC) on India's logistics sector, industrial growth, and export competitiveness.
Discuss the challenges encountered in the implementation of large-scale infrastructure projects like DFCs and how they are being addressed.
How do dedicated freight corridors contribute to India's broader vision of becoming a global manufacturing and trade hub?
articles.imageDescription: A long freight train moving rapidly on a dedicated track, symbolizing efficiency.
articles.trivia: The Dedicated Freight Corridor project is one of the largest railway infrastructure projects undertaken in India since independence.

articles.headline: Indigenous mRNA Vaccine for Dengue Shows Promising Results in Clinical Trials
articles.explainer:
• An Indian biotechnology firm announced promising results from Phase II clinical trials of its indigenously developed mRNA vaccine for dengue.
• The vaccine demonstrated high efficacy and a favorable safety profile across different serotypes of the dengue virus.
• This breakthrough could be a game-changer in combating dengue, a major public health challenge in tropical and subtropical regions, including India.
• The development aligns with India's push for self-reliance in vaccine development and biomedical research.
• Further trials are planned, and if successful, the vaccine could be a significant step towards global dengue prevention efforts.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: What are mRNA vaccines and how do they work?
Different serotypes of dengue virus.
Major vector-borne diseases prevalent in India.
Government initiatives for disease control (e.g., National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of indigenous mRNA vaccine development for diseases like dengue in strengthening India's public health infrastructure and vaccine diplomacy.
Analyze the challenges in developing and deploying vaccines for vector-borne diseases in tropical countries.
How can scientific research and technological innovation contribute to addressing major health challenges in India?
articles.imageDescription: Microscopic view of mRNA molecules and a representation of the dengue virus.
articles.trivia: Dengue is transmitted by the Aedes aegypti mosquito, which also transmits Zika, chikungunya, and yellow fever viruses.

articles.headline: IMD Develops Advanced Early Warning System for Cyclones and Heatwaves, Enhancing Disaster Preparedness
articles.explainer:
• The India Meteorological Department (IMD) announced the operationalization of an advanced multi-hazard early warning system, capable of providing more accurate and timely forecasts for cyclones and heatwaves.
• The new system integrates cutting-edge satellite imagery, supercomputing models, and ground-based observations to improve prediction accuracy and lead time.
• This enhancement will significantly boost India's disaster preparedness and response capabilities, minimizing loss of life and property.
• The IMD also plans to expand the reach of these warnings through various communication channels, including mobile apps and local community networks.
• The initiative underscores India's commitment to strengthening its disaster management framework, aligning with global efforts to build resilience against extreme weather events.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Role and functions of IMD.
Types of extreme weather events common in India (cyclones, heatwaves, floods, droughts).
National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) and its mandate.
Technological advancements used in weather forecasting (satellites, supercomputers).
Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction.
mainsPoints: Discuss the importance of robust early warning systems in mitigating the impact of extreme weather events like cyclones and heatwaves in India.
Analyze how technological advancements, such as satellite imagery and supercomputing, have transformed disaster preparedness and response capabilities.
Suggest measures to further enhance the effectiveness and reach of early warning systems, particularly for vulnerable communities.
articles.imageDescription: A weather satellite orbiting Earth, with weather patterns and warning symbols.
articles.trivia: India has significantly improved its cyclone forecasting and warning capabilities over the last decade, leading to a drastic reduction in cyclone-related fatalities.

articles.headline: Ancient Buddhist Site in Ladakh Nominated for UNESCO World Heritage Status
articles.explainer:
• An ancient Buddhist monastic complex in Ladakh has been formally nominated by India for inscription on the UNESCO World Heritage List.
• The site, known for its unique architecture, pristine murals, and continuous monastic tradition, represents a rich cultural and historical heritage of the Himalayan region.
• Its nomination highlights the universal value of Buddhist heritage and the cultural confluence along ancient trade routes.
• UNESCO recognition would bring increased international attention, funding for conservation, and promote sustainable tourism in Ladakh.
• This effort underscores India's commitment to preserving its diverse cultural heritage and showcasing it on the global stage.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: Current UNESCO World Heritage Sites in India (cultural, natural, mixed).
Key Buddhist sites in India (e.g., Sanchi, Bodh Gaya, Ajanta).
Features of Buddhist architecture (Stupa, Chaitya, Vihara).
Cultural significance of Ladakh region.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of nominating ancient Buddhist sites from Ladakh for UNESCO World Heritage status in terms of cultural preservation, promotion of tourism, and India's soft power.
Analyze the challenges involved in preserving and managing cultural heritage sites in remote and ecologically fragile regions like Ladakh.
Suggest strategies for leveraging UNESCO recognition to foster sustainable tourism and economic development for local communities while safeguarding cultural authenticity.
articles.imageDescription: An ancient Buddhist monastery nestled in the mountains of Ladakh.
articles.trivia: Ladakh is often called 'Little Tibet' due to its strong cultural ties with Tibetan Buddhism.

articles.headline: New E-Governance Initiative Launched to Enhance Public Service Delivery in Rural Areas
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Panchayati Raj launched a new e-governance initiative aimed at improving the delivery of public services and enhancing transparency in rural local bodies.
• The platform will digitize various government services, including land records, birth/death certificates, and social welfare scheme applications, making them accessible online.
• It seeks to bridge the digital divide by providing digital literacy training and establishing common service centers (CSCs) in remote villages.
• The initiative is expected to empower rural citizens, reduce corruption, and streamline administrative processes at the grassroots level.
• This is part of the broader 'Digital India' campaign, focusing on transforming governance through technology and ensuring inclusive development.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Panchayati Raj.
prelimsPoints: Key pillars of the Digital India program.
Role of Common Service Centers (CSCs) in rural areas.
73rd Constitutional Amendment Act and Panchayati Raj Institutions.
Examples of successful e-governance initiatives in India (e.g., UMANG app, DigiLocker).
mainsPoints: Evaluate the potential of e-governance initiatives in transforming public service delivery and enhancing transparency at the grassroots level in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing digital governance solutions in rural areas, particularly concerning digital literacy and infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure the inclusivity and effectiveness of e-governance platforms for empowering rural citizens and strengthening Panchayati Raj Institutions.
articles.imageDescription: A hand holding a smartphone displaying e-governance services, with a rural background.
articles.trivia: The e-Panchayat Mission Mode Project (MMP) aims to transform the functioning of Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) through technology.

articles.headline: India Achieves Significant Milestone in Renewable Energy Capacity Addition, Surpasses Target
articles.explainer:
• India announced a significant achievement in its renewable energy sector, surpassing its target for capacity addition for the current fiscal year.
• The country added a record amount of solar and wind power capacity, driven by supportive government policies and private sector investment.
• This milestone is crucial for India's commitment to achieving its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement and its long-term net-zero goals.
• The accelerated growth in renewables contributes to energy security, reduces carbon emissions, and creates green job opportunities.
• Challenges remain in grid integration, energy storage, and financing for further scaling up renewable energy deployment.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: India's renewable energy targets (e.g., 500 GW by 2030).
Different types of renewable energy sources (solar, wind, hydro, biomass).
Government schemes promoting renewable energy (e.g., PM-KUSUM).
Role of International Solar Alliance (ISA).
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's accelerated growth in renewable energy capacity and its implications for energy security and climate action.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in integrating a high share of intermittent renewable energy into the national grid.
Suggest policy interventions and technological advancements required to achieve India's ambitious long-term renewable energy and net-zero goals.
articles.imageDescription: Solar panels and wind turbines against a clear sky, symbolizing renewable energy.
articles.trivia: India is the third-largest producer of renewable energy in the world.

articles.headline: India Dispatches Another Consignment of Humanitarian Aid to Afghanistan Amidst Winter Crisis
articles.explainer:
• India dispatched a fresh consignment of humanitarian aid, including food grains, medicines, and winter clothing, to Afghanistan, addressing the severe winter crisis.
• This aid package reiterates India's commitment to supporting the Afghan people through challenging times, despite the complex geopolitical situation.
• The assistance is delivered through UN agencies and Afghan Red Crescent, ensuring equitable distribution to vulnerable populations.
• India has consistently provided humanitarian assistance to Afghanistan, focusing on food security, health, and education.
• The ongoing aid reflects India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and its historical ties with Afghanistan, emphasizing people-to-people connections.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India's major projects in Afghanistan (e.g., Salma Dam, Afghan Parliament).
Significance of Chabahar Port for India's connectivity to Afghanistan.
Current humanitarian situation in Afghanistan.
India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's humanitarian diplomacy towards Afghanistan and its strategic significance in the context of regional stability and India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy.
Analyze the challenges faced by India in extending aid and maintaining its influence in Afghanistan given the current political landscape.
How does humanitarian assistance contribute to India's soft power and its role as a responsible global actor?
articles.imageDescription: Aid packages being loaded onto a truck with Indian and Afghan flags.
articles.trivia: India has completed over 400 projects in Afghanistan, including in infrastructure, education, and health.

articles.headline: Government Unveils New Policy to Boost Semiconductor Manufacturing and Design Ecosystem
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government unveiled a comprehensive new policy aimed at significantly boosting semiconductor manufacturing and design capabilities within India.
• The policy offers enhanced fiscal incentives, production-linked schemes, and support for R&D to attract global and domestic players.
• It focuses on creating a robust semiconductor ecosystem, from chip design to fabrication and packaging, critical for India's technological sovereignty.
• The initiative is crucial for reducing India's reliance on imported semiconductors, which are vital for electronics, automobiles, and defence sectors.
• This strategic move aligns with the 'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' vision and aims to position India as a global hub for semiconductor production.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Mobilization of resources.
articles.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology
prelimsPoints: What are semiconductors and their importance?
Global semiconductor supply chain issues.
Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme and its objectives.
Key players in the global semiconductor industry.
India's current position in electronics manufacturing.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the new semiconductor policy in fostering India's technological self-reliance and economic growth.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for India in establishing a robust domestic semiconductor manufacturing and design ecosystem.
Evaluate how this policy can contribute to India's 'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' vision and its role in global technology supply chains.
articles.imageDescription: A close-up of a silicon wafer with integrated circuits, representing semiconductor technology.
articles.trivia: The global semiconductor industry is projected to reach a trillion-dollar valuation by 2030.

highlights: Under-construction tunnel of Delhi-Mumbai Expressway collapses in Kota, killing one and raising infrastructure safety concerns.
Supreme Court reinforces Right to Privacy in digital age, issues guidelines on state surveillance and data handling.
India's Q2 GDP growth surpasses projections at 7.8%, signaling strong economic recovery driven by manufacturing and services.
India and France deepen strategic partnership with new agreements in defence, space, and clean energy sectors.
ISRO successfully conducts critical test for next-gen Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology, advancing space capabilities.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Delhi-Mumbai Expressway:
1. It is India's longest greenfield expressway.
2. It connects the national capital to the financial capital, passing through five states.
3. The project is primarily executed by the Public Works Department (PWD).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Delhi-Mumbai Expressway is designed to be India's longest greenfield expressway. Statement 2 is correct: It connects Delhi and Mumbai, passing through Haryana, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, and Maharashtra. Statement 3 is incorrect: The project is primarily executed by the National Highways Authority of India (NHAI), not PWD.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following principles is most closely associated with India's stand on climate finance at international forums like COP29?
options: (b) Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)
prelimsMocks.explanation: India consistently advocates for the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) at international climate forums. This principle acknowledges that all countries have a shared responsibility to address climate change but recognizes that developed countries, due to their historical emissions and greater financial/technological capacities, should bear a greater burden.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Anti-Defection Law in India, consider the following statements:
1. It was introduced by the 52nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1985.
2. The decision on disqualification due to defection is made by the President in consultation with the Election Commission.
3. The law allows for defection in cases of a split in a political party, provided one-third of the members defect.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Anti-Defection Law was added as the Tenth Schedule to the Constitution by the 52nd Amendment Act of 1985. Statement 2 is incorrect: The decision on disqualification is made by the presiding officer of the House (Speaker/Chairman), not the President. Statement 3 is incorrect: The provision for a 'split' (where one-third of members could defect without disqualification) was removed by the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003. Only a 'merger' (with two-thirds of members) is now exempted.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of mRNA vaccines?
options: (a) They use a weakened or inactivated form of the virus.
(b) They instruct human cells to make a protein piece of the virus.
(c) They do not contain the live virus.
(d) They trigger an immune response that produces antibodies.
prelimsMocks.answer: (a) They use a weakened or inactivated form of the virus.
prelimsMocks.explanation: mRNA vaccines do not use a weakened or inactivated form of the virus. Instead, they use messenger RNA (mRNA) to instruct cells to produce a harmless piece of a viral protein, which then triggers an immune response. Options (b), (c), and (d) are all characteristic features of mRNA vaccines.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'PM-POSHAN+' scheme, recently approved by the Union Cabinet, is an enhanced version of which existing government program?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 'PM-POSHAN+' scheme is an upgraded and enhanced version of the Mid-Day Meal Scheme, which was renamed as PM POSHAN (Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Nirman) Scheme in 2021. It aims to extend coverage and provide holistic nutritional support to school children.

mainsMocks.question: The collapse of an under-construction tunnel of the Delhi-Mumbai Expressway highlights critical gaps in infrastructure safety and project implementation. Discuss the major challenges in ensuring quality and safety standards in India's large-scale infrastructure projects and suggest comprehensive measures to address them. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.; Disaster and disaster management.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the issue with the Kota incident. Discuss challenges: regulatory oversight, contractor accountability, skilled workforce, geological surveys, material quality, emergency response. Suggest measures: stringent safety audits, use of technology (AI/IoT), independent third-party inspections, skill development, robust legal framework, public-private partnership (PPP) accountability, and rapid disaster response protocols.

mainsMocks.question: India's rapidly growing digital economy necessitates a robust framework for balancing individual privacy with national security concerns. In light of recent judicial pronouncements, critically examine the challenges in achieving this balance and propose legislative and technological solutions. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure; Functioning of the Judiciary; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; Basics of cyber security.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with the Supreme Court's emphasis on privacy. Discuss the conflict: fundamental right to privacy vs. state's need for surveillance (e.g., terrorism, cybercrime). Challenges: lack of clear legal framework, technological advancements outpacing law, opaque surveillance procedures, potential for misuse. Solutions: comprehensive data protection law (DPDP Act implementation), independent oversight body for surveillance, judicial review of surveillance orders, privacy-by-design principles, public awareness, and international cooperation.

mainsMocks.question: India's ambitious target to become a global hub for semiconductor manufacturing is crucial for its technological sovereignty and economic growth. Analyze the opportunities and challenges in building a robust indigenous semiconductor ecosystem and suggest a roadmap for achieving self-reliance in this critical sector. (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the importance of semiconductors for India (Aatmanirbhar Bharat, critical technologies). Opportunities: large domestic market, skilled workforce, government incentives (PLI). Challenges: high capital investment, complex technology, global supply chain dominance, lack of advanced fabrication facilities, R&D intensity. Roadmap: targeted fiscal incentives, skill development, international collaborations, promoting R&D, attracting FDI, and developing design capabilities alongside manufacturing.

quickBytes.text: The Delhi-Mumbai Expressway is set to be India's longest greenfield expressway, reducing travel time significantly.
The Right to Privacy was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 by the Supreme Court in the K.S. Puttaswamy judgment (2017).
India's GDP growth is measured by the National Statistical Office (NSO) under the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation.
France was the first country to sign a strategic partnership with India, established in 1998.
ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) program aims to significantly reduce the cost of space launches.
The principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) originates from the Rio Declaration of 1992.
The Anti-Defection Law is enshrined in the Tenth Schedule of the Indian Constitution.
Dengue fever is a vector-borne disease primarily transmitted by the Aedes aegypti mosquito.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) is the apex body for disaster management in India.
India aims to achieve 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.

GS1.headline: Ancient Buddhist Site in Ladakh Nominated for UNESCO World Heritage Status
GS1.explainer:
• An ancient Buddhist monastic complex in Ladakh has been formally nominated by India for inscription on the UNESCO World Heritage List.
• The site, known for its unique architecture, pristine murals, and continuous monastic tradition, represents a rich cultural and historical heritage of the Himalayan region.
• Its nomination highlights the universal value of Buddhist heritage and the cultural confluence along ancient trade routes.
• UNESCO recognition would bring increased international attention, funding for conservation, and promote sustainable tourism in Ladakh.
• This effort underscores India's commitment to preserving its diverse cultural heritage and showcasing it on the global stage.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: Current UNESCO World Heritage Sites in India (cultural, natural, mixed).
Key Buddhist sites in India (e.g., Sanchi, Bodh Gaya, Ajanta).
Features of Buddhist architecture (Stupa, Chaitya, Vihara).
Cultural significance of Ladakh region.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of nominating ancient Buddhist sites from Ladakh for UNESCO World Heritage status in terms of cultural preservation, promotion of tourism, and India's soft power.
Analyze the challenges involved in preserving and managing cultural heritage sites in remote and ecologically fragile regions like Ladakh.
Suggest strategies for leveraging UNESCO recognition to foster sustainable tourism and economic development for local communities while safeguarding cultural authenticity.
GS1.imageDescription: An ancient Buddhist monastery nestled in the mountains of Ladakh.
GS1.trivia: Ladakh is often called 'Little Tibet' due to its strong cultural ties with Tibetan Buddhism.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Reinforces Right to Privacy in Digital Age, Issues Guidelines on State Surveillance
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, reiterated the fundamental right to privacy, extending its scope to cover digital data and online activities.
• The judgment emphasized that any state-sanctioned surveillance or data collection must meet strict tests of legality, necessity, and proportionality.
• While acknowledging national security concerns, the court stressed the need for robust oversight mechanisms to prevent arbitrary infringement of privacy.
• The ruling comes amidst growing debates over data protection laws and the balance between individual liberties and state powers in the digital era.
• It calls for a re-evaluation of existing legal frameworks governing surveillance and data handling by government agencies.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functioning of the Judiciary
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: K.S. Puttaswamy judgment (2017) and its significance.
Article 21 of the Indian Constitution (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
Key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on the balance between national security and individual privacy in India's digital landscape.
Discuss the challenges in implementing effective data protection and surveillance oversight mechanisms in a technologically evolving society.
Analyze how this judgment could influence future government policies on digital governance and data regulation.
GS2.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with a digital overlay representing data privacy.
GS2.trivia: The 'Right to be Forgotten' is an aspect of the right to privacy, allowing individuals to request the removal of personal information from the internet under certain conditions.

GS2.headline: India and France Bolster Strategic Partnership with Focus on Defence, Space, and Clean Energy
GS2.explainer:
• Prime Minister of India concluded a successful state visit to France, resulting in several key agreements to deepen the bilateral strategic partnership.
• Major outcomes include enhanced cooperation in defence manufacturing and technology transfer, particularly in naval and aerospace sectors.
• Both nations committed to expanding collaboration in space exploration, including joint missions and satellite technology development.
• Agreements were also signed in the areas of clean energy transition, artificial intelligence, and cybersecurity.
• The visit underscored the shared vision of a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, with emphasis on multilateralism and rules-based international order.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Major defence deals between India and France (e.g., Rafale, Scorpene submarines).
India's key strategic partners in Europe.
Significance of Indo-Pacific concept for India and France.
International Solar Alliance (ISA) and India-France collaboration.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the significance of the India-France strategic partnership in enhancing India's defence capabilities and promoting its foreign policy objectives.
Discuss how India's engagement with France contributes to regional stability in the Indo-Pacific and addresses global challenges like climate change.
Analyze the potential for future cooperation between India and France in emerging technologies and multilateral forums.
GS2.imageDescription: Prime Minister of India and President of France shaking hands with flags in the background.
GS2.trivia: France was the first country to sign a strategic partnership with India in 1998.

GS2.headline: Union Cabinet Approves 'PM-POSHAN+' Scheme for Enhanced Nutritional Support to School Children
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet gave its approval for the 'PM-POSHAN+' scheme, an upgraded version of the existing Mid-Day Meal program, focusing on holistic nutritional support for school children.
• The scheme aims to extend its coverage to pre-primary children and incorporate fortified food items to address micronutrient deficiencies.
• It emphasizes nutritional gardens in schools, cooking competitions, and community participation to promote healthy eating habits.
• PM-POSHAN+ also integrates health check-ups and nutritional counseling, linking directly with broader public health initiatives.
• This enhanced scheme is critical in combating malnutrition and improving educational outcomes, aligning with India's Sustainable Development Goals.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Poverty and developmental issues.
prelimsPoints: Objectives and features of the original Mid-Day Meal Scheme (now PM-POSHAN).
Key indicators of child malnutrition in India (stunting, wasting, underweight).
Government initiatives for women and child development.
SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) and SDG 4 (Quality Education).
mainsPoints: Evaluate the effectiveness of the Mid-Day Meal program in addressing child malnutrition and promoting school enrollment in India. How does PM-POSHAN+ aim to improve upon it?
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in combating malnutrition among school children and the role of inter-sectoral convergence in achieving better outcomes.
Suggest innovative approaches for strengthening community participation and ensuring sustainable nutritional support programs in schools.
GS2.imageDescription: School children enjoying a nutritious meal in a classroom setting.
GS2.trivia: The Mid-Day Meal Scheme was renamed as PM POSHAN (Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Nirman) Scheme in 2021.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Comprehensive Reforms in Anti-Defection Law to Ensure Political Stability
GS2.explainer:
• A high-level Parliamentary Standing Committee submitted its report recommending significant amendments to the Anti-Defection Law (Tenth Schedule of the Constitution).
• Key recommendations include clarifying the definition of 'voluntary giving up membership' and 'defiance of party whip' to reduce ambiguity.
• The panel suggested a time-bound decision-making process for the Speaker/Chairman on defection petitions, possibly involving the Election Commission or Supreme Court.
• The reforms aim to curb frequent party switching and ensure greater political stability, while also protecting the democratic rights of elected representatives.
• The report sparked renewed debate on the balance between party discipline and legislative freedom, emphasizing the need for robust institutional checks.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: Provisions of the Tenth Schedule of the Indian Constitution.
52nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1985).
Role of the Speaker/Chairman in anti-defection cases.
Exceptions to the anti-defection law (merger).
Kihoto Hollohan vs. Zachillhu case (1992).
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the effectiveness of the Anti-Defection Law in promoting political stability and ensuring party discipline in India. What are its major shortcomings?
Discuss the recommendations made by the Parliamentary Panel for reforming the Anti-Defection Law. How might these changes impact the functioning of Indian democracy?
Evaluate the role of the Speaker/Chairman in deciding defection cases and suggest measures to ensure impartiality and timely disposal of such petitions.
GS2.imageDescription: Indian Parliament building with a gavel, representing legislative reforms.
GS2.trivia: The Anti-Defection Law was enacted to prevent political defections that often led to instability in governments, especially at the state level.

GS2.headline: New E-Governance Initiative Launched to Enhance Public Service Delivery in Rural Areas
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Panchayati Raj launched a new e-governance initiative aimed at improving the delivery of public services and enhancing transparency in rural local bodies.
• The platform will digitize various government services, including land records, birth/death certificates, and social welfare scheme applications, making them accessible online.
• It seeks to bridge the digital divide by providing digital literacy training and establishing common service centers (CSCs) in remote villages.
• The initiative is expected to empower rural citizens, reduce corruption, and streamline administrative processes at the grassroots level.
• This is part of the broader 'Digital India' campaign, focusing on transforming governance through technology and ensuring inclusive development.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Panchayati Raj.
prelimsPoints: Key pillars of the Digital India program.
Role of Common Service Centers (CSCs) in rural areas.
73rd Constitutional Amendment Act and Panchayati Raj Institutions.
Examples of successful e-governance initiatives in India (e.g., UMANG app, DigiLocker).
mainsPoints: Evaluate the potential of e-governance initiatives in transforming public service delivery and enhancing transparency at the grassroots level in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing digital governance solutions in rural areas, particularly concerning digital literacy and infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure the inclusivity and effectiveness of e-governance platforms for empowering rural citizens and strengthening Panchayati Raj Institutions.
GS2.imageDescription: A hand holding a smartphone displaying e-governance services, with a rural background.
GS2.trivia: The e-Panchayat Mission Mode Project (MMP) aims to transform the functioning of Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) through technology.

GS2.headline: India Dispatches Another Consignment of Humanitarian Aid to Afghanistan Amidst Winter Crisis
GS2.explainer:
• India dispatched a fresh consignment of humanitarian aid, including food grains, medicines, and winter clothing, to Afghanistan, addressing the severe winter crisis.
• This aid package reiterates India's commitment to supporting the Afghan people through challenging times, despite the complex geopolitical situation.
• The assistance is delivered through UN agencies and Afghan Red Crescent, ensuring equitable distribution to vulnerable populations.
• India has consistently provided humanitarian assistance to Afghanistan, focusing on food security, health, and education.
• The ongoing aid reflects India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and its historical ties with Afghanistan, emphasizing people-to-people connections.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India's major projects in Afghanistan (e.g., Salma Dam, Afghan Parliament).
Significance of Chabahar Port for India's connectivity to Afghanistan.
Current humanitarian situation in Afghanistan.
India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's humanitarian diplomacy towards Afghanistan and its strategic significance in the context of regional stability and India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy.
Analyze the challenges faced by India in extending aid and maintaining its influence in Afghanistan given the current political landscape.
How does humanitarian assistance contribute to India's soft power and its role as a responsible global actor?
GS2.imageDescription: Aid packages being loaded onto a truck with Indian and Afghan flags.
GS2.trivia: India has completed over 400 projects in Afghanistan, including in infrastructure, education, and health.

GS3.headline: Under-Construction Delhi-Mumbai Expressway Tunnel Collapses in Kota, Raising Infrastructure Safety Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• At least one person was killed and three others injured in the collapse of an under-construction tunnel section of the Delhi–Mumbai Expressway in Kota, Rajasthan.
• The incident highlights critical issues regarding safety protocols, quality control, and supervision in large-scale infrastructure projects across the country.
• Authorities have initiated an inquiry to ascertain the cause, including potential lapses in design, construction practices, or material quality.
• The Delhi-Mumbai Expressway is a flagship project aimed at reducing travel time and boosting economic activity across several states.
• This incident brings to the forefront the need for stringent regulatory oversight and comprehensive risk assessment in major public works.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Disaster and disaster management

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention
prelimsPoints: Delhi-Mumbai Expressway: Key features, states covered (Rajasthan, MP, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Haryana).
NHAI: Mandate and role in highway development.
Disaster Management Act, 2005: Provisions for accident response.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in ensuring safety standards in large-scale infrastructure projects in India, using the Kota incident as a case study.
Analyze the role of regulatory bodies and project contractors in maintaining quality and safety during construction.
Suggest measures to improve disaster preparedness and response mechanisms for construction site accidents.
GS3.imageDescription: Collapsed section of an under-construction tunnel of the Delhi-Mumbai Expressway.
GS3.trivia: The Delhi-Mumbai Expressway, once completed, will be India's longest greenfield expressway, reducing travel time between Delhi and Mumbai from 24 hours to 12 hours.

GS3.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Surpasses Projections, Signals Strong Economic Recovery
GS3.explainer:
• India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) registered a robust growth rate of 7.8% in the second quarter (July-September) of FY 2024-25, exceeding market expectations.
• The growth was primarily driven by strong performance in the manufacturing, construction, and services sectors, indicating broad-based recovery.
• Private consumption and government expenditure also contributed significantly to the economic expansion.
• This positive trend strengthens India's position as one of the fastest-growing major economies globally, despite prevailing global headwinds.
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and various international agencies are likely to revise their full-year growth forecasts upwards.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
GS3.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation
prelimsPoints: Definition and components of GDP and GVA.
Role of CSO (now NSO) in GDP calculation.
Current trends in key economic indicators (inflation, industrial production).
Impact of global factors on India's economy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's strong Q2 GDP growth and discuss its sustainability in the current global economic scenario.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India to maintain its growth momentum and achieve its long-term economic objectives.
Discuss the implications of this growth on employment generation, poverty reduction, and investment climate in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Financial graph showing an upward trend, representing India's GDP growth.
GS3.trivia: India's economy is projected to become the third-largest in the world by 2027, surpassing Germany and Japan.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Critical Test for Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle Technology
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful completion of a crucial experimental test flight for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) program.
• The test involved a scaled-down prototype demonstrating autonomous landing capabilities under simulated operational conditions.
• This achievement marks a significant step towards developing India's own two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) reusable launch system, aiming to drastically reduce space mission costs.
• RLV technology is considered vital for future space exploration, satellite deployment, and potential space tourism.
• ISRO aims to achieve full operational capability for its RLV by the end of the decade, enhancing India's competitive edge in the global space market.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Difference between expendable and reusable launch vehicles.
Key ISRO missions (Gaganyaan, Chandrayaan, Mangalyaan).
Role of IN-SPACe and NSIL in India's space sector.
Benefits of reusable launch technology (cost reduction, environmental impact).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of developing Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology for India's space program and its implications for global space competitiveness.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with indigenous development of advanced space technologies like RLV.
How can India leverage its advancements in space technology to foster economic growth and contribute to sustainable development goals?
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle prototype landing on a runway.
GS3.trivia: The first experimental flight of ISRO's RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) was conducted in 2016, successfully demonstrating the hypersonic flight regime.

GS3.headline: Government Launches National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture to Boost Farmers' Adaptability
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government officially launched the 'National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture' (NMCRA) aimed at enhancing the adaptability of Indian agriculture to climate change.
• The mission focuses on developing and promoting climate-smart agricultural practices, including drought-resistant crop varieties, efficient water management, and sustainable land use.
• It will integrate traditional knowledge with modern scientific approaches to build resilience across various agro-climatic zones.
• NMCRA also includes components for capacity building among farmers, providing access to climate information services, and promoting agroforestry.
• The initiative is crucial for ensuring food security and farmers' livelihoods in the face of increasing climate variability and extreme weather events.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
keyTerms: National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)
prelimsPoints: Eight Missions under NAPCC (National Action Plan on Climate Change).
Key government initiatives for agricultural development (e.g., PM-KISAN, PM Fasal Bima Yojana).
Concepts of 'climate-smart village' and 'precision agriculture'.
Different types of irrigation systems in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the National Mission for Climate-Resilient Agriculture in addressing the challenges posed by climate change to India's food security and agricultural sector.
Analyze the potential impact of climate-smart agricultural practices on farmers' income and environmental sustainability.
Suggest policy measures and technological interventions required to ensure widespread adoption and success of climate-resilient agriculture in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with modern agricultural technology and diverse crops.
GS3.trivia: India is highly vulnerable to climate change, with agriculture being one of the most susceptible sectors, impacting over 58% of the rural population.

GS3.headline: Indian Army Inducts Advanced Indigenous Artillery Systems, Boosting 'Make in India' in Defence
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Army formally inducted a new batch of indigenously developed advanced artillery gun systems, marking a significant milestone for 'Make in India' in defence.
• These new systems, designed and manufactured by Indian firms, feature enhanced range, accuracy, and mobility, crucial for modern warfare scenarios.
• The induction is part of the ongoing modernization drive of the Indian armed forces to reduce reliance on foreign imports and achieve self-reliance in defence production.
• This move is expected to boost the domestic defence industrial base, create jobs, and foster technological advancements within the country.
• The government reiterated its commitment to indigenous defence procurement and exports, positioning India as a global defence manufacturing hub.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Key indigenous defence platforms (e.g., LCA Tejas, Arjun Tank, Akash missile).
Defence corridors in India.
Recent policy changes to boost domestic defence manufacturing.
Role of DRDO in defence R&D.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of indigenous defence production for India's national security and strategic autonomy.
Discuss the challenges faced by India in achieving complete self-reliance in defence manufacturing and suggest measures to overcome them.
Evaluate the impact of 'Make in India' initiatives on the modernization of the Indian armed forces and the growth of the domestic defence industry.
GS3.imageDescription: New indigenous artillery guns being showcased by the Indian Army.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the largest importers of defence equipment globally, but the 'Make in India' initiative aims to reverse this trend.

GS3.headline: Government's PSU Disinvestment Drive Gains Momentum, New Assets Listed for Strategic Sale
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government announced accelerated progress in its Public Sector Undertaking (PSU) disinvestment program, listing several new state-owned assets for strategic sale.
• The move is aimed at unlocking value from government holdings, generating revenue for public welfare schemes, and improving the efficiency and competitiveness of PSUs.
• Key sectors identified for disinvestment include manufacturing, financial services, and logistics, with a focus on non-strategic enterprises.
• The government reiterated its commitment to minimal presence in strategic sectors while privatizing non-strategic ones, as part of its new Public Sector Enterprise Policy.
• Challenges remain in market valuation, investor interest, and employee concerns, requiring careful policy execution and stakeholder management.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
GS3.source: Department of Investment and Public Asset Management (DIPAM)
prelimsPoints: Definition of disinvestment and privatization.
Objectives of government's disinvestment policy.
Role of DIPAM (Department of Investment and Public Asset Management).
Concept of strategic vs. non-strategic sectors.
mainsPoints: Critically examine the rationale behind the government's accelerated PSU disinvestment drive and its potential impact on the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges and criticisms associated with privatization and strategic sale of public sector enterprises in India.
Suggest measures to ensure transparency, maximize value realization, and address employee concerns during the disinvestment process.
GS3.imageDescription: Indian currency notes stacked on a table, symbolizing revenue generation from disinvestment.
GS3.trivia: The first major disinvestment in India took place in 1991-92, marking a shift in economic policy.

GS3.headline: India Reiterates Call for Equitable Climate Finance and CBDR at COP29
GS3.explainer:
• At the ongoing COP29 climate summit, India strongly advocated for equitable climate finance and upheld the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC).
• India called upon developed nations to fulfill their financial commitments, emphasizing the need for predictable and adequate funding for climate action in developing countries.
• The Indian delegation highlighted its ambitious national targets for renewable energy and emissions reduction, despite being a developing economy.
• Discussions centered on mechanisms for technology transfer, capacity building, and adaptation finance for vulnerable nations.
• India stressed that climate justice is paramount and that the burden of climate action should not disproportionately fall on developing nations.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Location and key outcomes of COP29 (hypothetical, as COP29 is Nov 11-22, 2024).
Principle of CBDR-RC: its origin and significance.
India's NDC targets under the Paris Agreement.
Difference between mitigation and adaptation in climate action.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's stance on climate finance and the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) at international climate forums.
Analyze the challenges in achieving global consensus on climate finance and technology transfer, particularly between developed and developing nations.
Evaluate India's efforts and commitments towards climate action, balancing its development imperatives with environmental sustainability.
GS3.imageDescription: Delegates at a COP conference, with a globe overlay representing climate change.
GS3.trivia: The Paris Agreement (2015) was the first universal, legally binding global climate change agreement, replacing the Kyoto Protocol.

GS3.headline: Western Dedicated Freight Corridor Nearing Completion, Set to Transform Logistics Efficiency
GS3.explainer:
• The Western Dedicated Freight Corridor (WDFC), a crucial infrastructure project, is reportedly nearing its full operational completion, with final stretches expected to be commissioned soon.
• WDFC aims to provide a dedicated, high-speed freight rail network connecting the industrial hubs of northern India to the western ports (Jawaharlal Nehru Port, Vadodara, etc.).
• This corridor is expected to significantly reduce transit times, lower logistics costs, and decongest existing passenger rail lines.
• It will boost industrial growth, enhance export competitiveness, and facilitate faster movement of goods, contributing to India's economic development.
